chapters.title: You do not need fancy stores to eat vegan
chapters.text:
Toni Okamoto first went vegan in high school, where she ran on a steady diet of fast food. Right across the street sat a Taco Bell, and every lunch was a pair of beef tacos and a bean burrito.

After eating, she would head to track practice and push her body hard, and she would get sick to her stomach nearly every time. Her coach suggested cutting out the fast food and red meat to boost her performance. That was the start of a plant-based journey, and at first, health was the only reason she stuck with it.

Her interest grew in her early twenties when she took a few classes and joined a vegetarian club at her college. The group became her anchor. Members taught her new things and pushed her forward. They ran bake sales, hosted potlucks, and visited animal sanctuaries together. The community changed her. She stopped being vegan only for her body and started caring about animal welfare and the environment too.

You can eat wonderful plant-based food without stepping into a single expensive shop.

Her path to becoming a food-budget expert came from a different corner of her life. Between low-paying jobs, student loans, vet bills, broken-down cars, and credit card debt, she lived paycheck to paycheck for years. She would look at her expenses and watch the grocery money sink to the bottom of the list, knowing that instant ramen beat eviction every time.

Her family had never been big on food spending, so when she went vegan in her twenties, they worried she would drain her money on the new lifestyle. Plant-Based on a Budget was born from her effort to eat well even when cash was tight.

In this summary, you will learn that vegan eating does not require expensive stores. You can shop at the most affordable markets and still enjoy rich, tasty food every day.

chapters.title: Quick breakfasts get the day off to a strong start
chapters.text:
Starting the day with a solid meal matters a lot. Breakfast is often called the most important meal for good reason. These five-ingredient peanut butter bites come together fast and work well for a busy morning.

The ingredients are one teaspoon of canola or vegetable oil, half a cup of raisins, one cup of hot water, two ripe bananas, one cup of peanut butter (creamy or chunky), and two-thirds of a cup of old-fashioned rolled oats. For an optional touch, add one-third cup of raisins or dried cranberries after step four.

To make them, preheat the oven to 350 degrees and lightly grease a muffin tin. Soak the raisins in hot water for two minutes, then drain them. In a high-powered blender or food processor, combine the softened raisins, bananas, peanut butter, and oats, and blend for about a minute until smooth. Spoon the batter into the muffin tin, filling each cup halfway. Bake for seventeen to twenty minutes, until the tops turn golden.

You do not need fancy tools to get started in the kitchen.

Next up, overnight oats. This recipe makes one to two servings and uses one cup of old-fashioned rolled oats, three-quarters of a cup of frozen berries, two tablespoons of raw sunflower seeds without shells, one cup of plant-based milk, and one sliced banana. Optional toppings include a teaspoon of maple syrup, a teaspoon of brown sugar, a teaspoon and a half of cinnamon, a teaspoon and a half of chia seeds, and a tablespoon of coconut flakes.

Combine the oats, berries, sunflower seeds, and milk in a glass, storage container, or mason jar. Cover with foil and refrigerate overnight. In the morning, stir it up and enjoy cold. No cooking is needed, since the oats soften on their own. Top with the sliced banana to sweeten.

Then there is breakfast quinoa, which serves two to four. You can stir in coconut shreds, raisins, dried cranberries, or mashed banana if you want it sweeter. The ingredients are two cups of plant-based milk, one cup of quinoa, half a cup of frozen berries, a teaspoon and a half of cinnamon, and one sliced banana. Optional toppings include walnuts or almonds, raw sunflower seeds without shells, coconut shreds, and brown sugar.

Bring the milk to a boil in a medium pot over medium-high heat. Lower the heat, add the quinoa, and cover. Let it simmer for fifteen minutes. Add the frozen berries and cinnamon, stir, cover, and simmer for five more minutes. Remove from heat and fluff with a spoon. Serve with sliced banana and any toppings you like.

chapters.title: Salads are simple, satisfying, and full of goodness
chapters.text:
Salads are easy to make and packed with nutrition. In a single bowl, you can cover a large slice of your daily needs and keep your energy steady through work, study, or anything else on your plate. These ideas show just how simple it can be.

Quinoa salad serves two to four. The list of ways to dress up quinoa is long. You can toss in any kind of olives, chopped spinach or kale, garlic, finely chopped steamed carrots, and more.

You will need three cups of quinoa cooked in vegetable broth, half a cup of chopped tomato, three-quarters of a cup of diced cucumber (about half a cucumber), three-quarters of a cup of diced yellow, white, or red onion (about one small onion), three-quarters of a cup of chopped green or kalamata olives, and three tablespoons of lemon juice (about one small lemon).

Let the quinoa cool, then move it to a large bowl. Add the tomato, onion, olives, cucumber, and lemon juice, and mix well.

Perfect potluck pasta salad serves six to eight. Gather one pound of bowtie pasta, two tablespoons of olive oil, three-quarters of a cup of diced yellow, white, or red onion, a teaspoon of minced garlic, one 6.5-ounce jar of marinated artichoke quarters with their juices, the juice of one lemon, half a teaspoon of minced lemon zest, a quarter teaspoon of salt, halved cherry tomatoes, freshly ground pepper to taste, and one can of chickpeas, drained and rinsed, to add in step three.

Boil water in a large pot over medium-high heat and cook the pasta. Drain and set it aside to cool. While it cools, heat the olive oil in a large pan, then saute the garlic and onion over medium heat until they soften. Add the artichokes with their juices, salt, lemon zest, and lemon juice, and simmer for five minutes. Stir in the drained pasta and toss to coat. Top with cherry tomatoes and a little pepper. If you are in a rush, serve warm. Otherwise, chill it for thirty minutes before serving.

It is fine to think carefully about the cost of your food.

Basic table salad serves two to four. You will need one head of romaine lettuce or spinach, coarsely chopped, half a cup of sliced red, white, or yellow onion, half to one cucumber cut in quarters, one cup of halved cherry or grape tomatoes, and two-thirds of a cup of sweet vinaigrette or your favorite dressing. Optional extras include one cup of pressed and diced extra-firm tofu, one chopped avocado, a sprinkle of nutritional yeast, and sunflower or pumpkin seeds, all added in step one.

Toss the lettuce, onion, cucumber, and tomatoes in a large bowl. Add the dressing and toss to coat.

chapters.title: Smart snacks keep hunger at bay and moods steady
chapters.text:
It happens to everyone. One minute, the day is rolling along, and the next, your stomach growls. You cannot focus, and every little thing starts to bother you. You are hangry, plain and simple.

These snacks will quiet a rumbly stomach and keep your energy up.

Homemade hummus starts with one 15-ounce can of chickpeas, drained and rinsed, two cloves of garlic, a quarter cup of tahini, two tablespoons of lemon juice, three tablespoons of water, a teaspoon of ground cumin, and salt to taste.

You can pick up store-bought hummus, but making it at home saves cash and cuts down on plastic waste.

Combine all the ingredients in a food processor and blend on high until completely smooth.

Nacho cheese makes about three cups. You will need two cups of peeled and diced russet potatoes, one cup of sliced carrots, half a cup of water, one tablespoon of lemon juice, half a cup of nutritional yeast, a teaspoon of onion powder, a teaspoon of garlic powder, and half a teaspoon of salt. For an optional twist, add a quarter cup of salsa in step two.

Place the carrots and potatoes in a large pot of water. Bring it to a boil over medium heat and cook for ten minutes, until the vegetables are soft. Drain and turn off the heat. Blend the boiled potatoes, onion powder, garlic powder, carrots, water, lemon juice, nutritional yeast, and salt until completely smooth.

Peanut butter balls make about twelve. The secret to their popularity is simple. They taste great and they use only three ingredients.

You will need one cup of old-fashioned rolled oats, half a cup of creamy peanut butter, and a quarter cup of raisins. Optional extras include a quarter cup of vegan semi-sweet mini chocolate chips, a quarter cup of coconut shreds, or a quarter cup of chopped nuts, all added in step one.

Combine all the ingredients in a large bowl and stir well with your hands. Roll the mixture into small balls, each about the size of a tablespoon, and place them on a baking sheet. Freeze for about thirty minutes. Eat right away or store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for four to five days.

chapters.title: Dinner brings the family together around good food
chapters.text:
Few things beat sitting down at the end of a long day with the people you love most. Sharing food is one of the best ways to reconnect and reset. These vitamin-rich dishes will put a smile on every face at the table.

Tofu with peanut sauce serves two. You will need half a cup of creamy peanut butter, two tablespoons of soy sauce, one tablespoon of lime juice, a teaspoon of diced garlic, a third of a cup of water, a quarter teaspoon of red chili flakes, and one block of tofu pressed and cut into half-inch cubes. Optional toppings include a teaspoon of sesame oil, half a teaspoon of grated ginger, a teaspoon of hot sauce, and a quarter cup of chopped unsalted roasted peanuts.

Whisk the lime juice, garlic, peanut butter, soy sauce, water, and red chili flakes in a large bowl until smooth and creamy. Add the tofu and toss to coat.

Tofu contains all nine essential amino acids and is a good source of protein, iron, calcium, and vitamin B1.

Fried brown rice serves four. You will need one tablespoon of sesame oil, half a cup of diced onion, a teaspoon of diced garlic, half a cup of frozen peas, half a cup of grated or finely diced carrots, two and a half cups of cooked brown rice, and one and a half to two tablespoons of soy sauce.

Heat the sesame oil in a large pan over medium-high heat. Add the onion, garlic, peas, and carrots, and saute for three minutes, until the onion turns translucent. Add the brown rice and soy sauce, and cook for five to eight minutes, stirring often.

Loaded sweet potatoes are next. You will need one can of pinto or black beans, one 15.25-ounce can of unsalted corn (drained and rinsed), three-quarters of a cup of canned diced tomatoes, a quarter cup of chopped green onion, two teaspoons of ground cumin, salt and pepper to taste, and two microwaved sweet potatoes cut in half.

Mix the beans, corn, tomatoes, green onion, cumin, salt, and pepper in a large bowl. Place the sweet potatoes on a plate and mash the insides with a spoon, leaving them in their skins. Spoon the bean mixture on top.

A study in the Oxford Academic Journal of Nutrition found that soy isoflavone, a natural compound in tofu, helps lower LDL cholesterol in the blood.

chapters.title: Sweet treats fit naturally into plant-based eating
chapters.text:
A common myth about healthy eating, especially a plant-based diet, is that you have to give up sweets. That could not be further from the truth. You can enjoy rich, sweet treats. Here is how.

Four-ingredient chocolate pie makes one pie. It only needs four ingredients, and you can grab every one of them at a regular grocery store.

You will need one 12-ounce bag of vegan semi-sweet chocolate chips, one 14-ounce block of silken (soft) tofu, drained, two teaspoons of vanilla extract, and one vegan graham-cracker pie crust.

Microwave the chocolate chips in a microwave-safe bowl for about 45 seconds. Stir with a fork until smooth. Blend the melted chocolate, tofu, and vanilla in a blender until creamy. Pour the mixture into the pie crust and freeze for thirty minutes, until the filling sets. You can store the pie in the refrigerator for up to five days.

Mixed berry cobbler makes nine servings. You will need a teaspoon of canola or vegetable oil, one 12-ounce bag of frozen mixed berries, two-thirds of a cup of granulated sugar, one cup of whole wheat or all-purpose flour, two teaspoons of baking powder, one cup of plant-based milk, and three tablespoons of unsweetened apple sauce.

Preheat the oven to 375 degrees and lightly grease an 8 by 8-inch baking pan. In a medium bowl, mix the frozen berries and sugar thoroughly. Set aside. In a separate large bowl, mix the flour, baking powder, milk, and apple sauce until smooth. Spread the batter in the pan and top with the berry and sugar mix. Bake for fifty minutes, until golden.

Strawberries are low in fat and calories yet rich in vitamins and antioxidants.

Chocolate covered strawberries make fifteen to twenty pieces. You will need one 12-ounce bag of vegan semi-sweet chocolate chips and one one-pound carton of strawberries, washed and dried.

Line a baking sheet with parchment paper. Place the chocolate chips in a bowl and microwave in thirty-second bursts, stirring in between, until the chocolate is silky. Hold each strawberry by its stem, dip it in chocolate, and set it on the prepared sheet. Refrigerate for twenty to twenty-five minutes, until the chocolate hardens. Keep the strawberries in the refrigerator until serving time.

chapters.title: A simple pantry makes weekly meal planning easy
chapters.text:
Plant-based grocery shopping sounds pricey, but it does not have to be. Most of what you need is probably already in your kitchen. You just have to stock the basics for the week ahead.

Quinoa makes about three cups. You will need one cup of quinoa and one and a half cups of water or vegetable broth.

Rinse the quinoa in cold water. Cook it with the broth or water in a pressure pot for about five minutes. Release the pressure, open the lid, and fluff the quinoa with a fork. Store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for four to five days.

Quinoa is non-GMO, gluten-free, and packed with protein, fiber, magnesium, manganese, and iron.

Brown rice makes about two cups. You will need one cup of brown rice and two cups of water.

Place the rice and water in a pressure cooker and cook on high pressure for twenty minutes. Remove the lid and fluff with a fork. Store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to four days.

Beans make about three cups. You will need one and a half cups of black beans, chickpeas, or pinto beans, rinsed, and five cups of water.

Place the beans and water in the pressure cooker and cook on high pressure. Release the pressure and remove the lid. Store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to five days.

Brown or green lentils make about two and a half cups. You will need one cup of lentils, rinsed, and one and a half cups of water or vegetable broth.

Place the lentils and water (or broth) in the pressure cooker and cook on high pressure for eight minutes. Release the pressure and remove the lid. Store in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to five days.

Green split peas make about two cups. You will need one cup of split peas, rinsed, and one and a half cups of water.

Place the split peas in a large pot of water with a lid. Bring to a boil over high heat. Reduce the heat to low and let it sit for forty-five minutes. Keep in the refrigerator for four to five days.

Croutons make four servings. You will need half a day-old baguette, sliced, two tablespoons of olive oil, and half a tablespoon of garlic salt.

Preheat the oven to 350 degrees. Brush the baguette slices with olive oil and sprinkle with garlic salt. Cut the bread into cubes, spread them on a baking sheet, and bake for ten to fifteen minutes, until golden brown. Let the croutons cool before serving. They taste best fresh out of the oven.

chapters.title: Final thoughts
chapters.text:
Toni Okamoto grew up in a neighborhood where most families did not have a lot of food money. Diet-related illnesses like type 2 diabetes and heart disease were common. When she discovered the power of a plant-based diet, she wanted to share it with her family, and her top priority was to present it in a familiar, flavorful way that answered their biggest concern, cost.

Okamoto wanted her family to eat well and stay healthy. She also knew they could not afford anything too fancy, even if it would have been a big boost to their health. So she rolled up her sleeves and figured out how to eat a strong plant-based diet and stay healthy without going broke.

As she learned, eating plant-based does not mean shopping at specialty stores or sipping a pricey superfood elixir. The supermarket you already visit likely has everything you need to treat your body and your budget well.

You never thought you could eat healthy meals for under two dollars, right? Think again. With the right ingredients and recipes, you can build your own dollar menu and leave the drive-through behind. Enjoy the benefits of plant-based eating. Good health should not be reserved for those who can pay the most, and none of us should suffer from preventable illnesses.

Whether you have a money tree or not, health is wealth, and this guide has given you one of the greatest gifts of wealth. You deserve it.

A few habits to try. Finish everything in your bowl. Do not waste food. Have a bit of tomato sauce left in the can? Freeze it and toss it into a soup later. Shop at the farmers' market, where deals are easy to find. Shop with a list, since shopping with intention cuts down on impulse buys and keeps you within your budget.
